description: Seed roster for the BlackHoleDS reasoning-lens XML profiles. Profiles are structured prompts, not claims about a person's mind. See docs/vision/SCIENTIFIC_INTEGRITY_CHARTER.md section VI.

contributions.title: Schwarzschild solution to Einstein's field equations
contributions.description: First exact non-trivial solution to Einstein's vacuum field equations. Describes the spacetime outside a non-rotating, uncharged, spherically symmetric mass.
contributions.relevance: Foundational. The Schwarzschild metric is the analytic baseline of this project.

contributions.title: Schwarzschild radius
contributions.description: Defined the characteristic radius r_s = 2GM/c^2 at which the metric becomes singular in Schwarzschild coordinates (the event horizon for non-rotating black holes).
contributions.relevance: Implemented in src/BlackHoleDS.cpp and tested in tests/smoke_tests.cpp.

reasoning.proof_standard: Find exact closed-form solutions when possible; verify by substitution into the original equations.
questions: What is the simplest geometry that still captures the essential physics?
Where does this solution break down, and what is happening there physically vs mathematically?
Can I separate variables exploiting the symmetry?
failures: Mistaking a coordinate singularity for a physical singularity (r = r_s vs r = 0).

bibliography.citation: Schwarzschild, K. (1916). Uber das Gravitationsfeld eines Massenpunktes nach der Einsteinschen Theorie. Sitzungsberichte der Preussischen Akademie der Wissenschaften.

apply.when: Designing or validating any module that touches non-rotating, vacuum-spacetime geometry.
apply.cautions: His original coordinate system has a coordinate singularity at r = r_s. Use Eddington-Finkelstein or Kruskal-Szekeres coordinates near the horizon.

contributions.title: Kerr solution
contributions.description: Exact axially symmetric vacuum solution to Einstein's field equations describing rotating black holes.
contributions.relevance: The other foundational metric in this project. Used in tests/smoke_tests.cpp via kerr_isco_radius.

reasoning.proof_standard: Algebraically special spacetimes (Petrov type D); guess-and-verify backed by symmetry.
questions: What symmetries can I exploit to reduce the field equations?
Is this spacetime algebraically special?
What conserved quantities does the symmetry guarantee?
failures: Ignoring the rich global structure of Kerr (multiple horizons, ergosphere, ring singularity, closed timelike curves inside the inner horizon).

bibliography.citation: Kerr, R. P. (1963). Gravitational field of a spinning mass as an example of algebraically special metrics. Phys. Rev. Lett. 11, 237.

apply.when: Designing modules that touch rotating black holes, ISCO, photon sphere with spin, frame dragging, or the ergosphere.
apply.cautions: The maximally extended Kerr spacetime includes pathologies inside the inner horizon. Validation suites should use astrophysically reasonable parameter ranges.

contributions.title: General relativity
contributions.description: Geometric theory of gravitation in which mass-energy curves spacetime. The Einstein field equations relate the Einstein tensor to the stress-energy tensor.
contributions.relevance: The entire physics layer of this project sits on top of Einstein's field equations.

contributions.title: Equivalence principle
contributions.description: Local equivalence of gravitation and acceleration; foundational for the geometric interpretation of gravity.
contributions.relevance: Drives the choice of coordinate systems and freely falling observers in the integrator.

reasoning.proof_standard: Thought experiments grounded in invariance principles; equations follow the geometry.
questions: What is the simplest universal principle that this phenomenon obeys?
What does a freely falling observer see?
How is the physics expressed in coordinate-independent terms?
failures: Letting an elegant idea outrun observational checks (Einstein's cosmological constant flip-flop, his resistance to indeterministic quantum mechanics).

bibliography.citation: Einstein, A. (1915). The field equations of gravitation. Sitzungsberichte der Preussischen Akademie der Wissenschaften, 844-847.

apply.when: Reviewing whether a proposed change respects general covariance, equivalence, and the structure of the field equations.
apply.cautions: Beauty is a strong heuristic but not a substitute for testable predictions. Tag elegant unverified ideas as speculative_extension.

contributions.title: Penrose singularity theorem
contributions.description: Proved that, under physically reasonable energy conditions, gravitational collapse must lead to a spacetime singularity.
contributions.relevance: Justifies treating black-hole interiors as places where the classical theory breaks down. Bounds the model_status of interior claims.

contributions.title: Penrose process
contributions.description: Energy extraction from a rotating black hole via the ergosphere.
contributions.relevance: Future work on Kerr energetics and accretion-disk models.

contributions.title: Penrose diagrams
contributions.description: Conformal compactifications used to visualize the global causal structure of spacetimes.
contributions.relevance: Useful for visualization_metaphor modules and for reasoning about horizons.

reasoning.proof_standard: Global topological and causal arguments rather than local coordinate computations.
questions: What does the global causal structure of this spacetime look like?
Does an energy condition fail anywhere?
Is there a trapped surface?
failures: Conflating mathematical idealizations (closed trapped surface, asymptotic flatness) with astrophysical realities without saying so.

bibliography.citation: Penrose, R. (1965). Gravitational collapse and space-time singularities. Phys. Rev. Lett. 14, 57.

apply.when: Reasoning about black-hole interiors, energy extraction, cosmological singularities, or whether a claim is global vs local.
apply.cautions: His later speculative work (cyclic conformal cosmology, Orch-OR consciousness) is not validated physics. Label any incursion of those ideas as speculative_extension.

profiles.name: Stephen Hawking
secondary_fields: Quantum field theory in curved spacetime

contributions.title: Hawking radiation
contributions.description: Predicted that black holes emit thermal radiation due to quantum effects near the event horizon, with temperature T = hbar c^3 / (8 pi G M k_B).
contributions.relevance: Defines the speculative_extension boundary for this project. Hawking radiation is well-established theoretically but unobserved.

contributions.title: Four laws of black hole mechanics
contributions.description: Co-developed (with Bardeen and Carter) the formal analogy between black hole mechanics and the laws of thermodynamics.
contributions.relevance: Anchors the connection between geometry (surface gravity, horizon area) and thermodynamic-style quantities (temperature, entropy).

contributions.title: Penrose-Hawking singularity theorems
contributions.description: Generalized Penrose's theorem to broader contexts including cosmological singularities.
contributions.relevance: Same as Penrose's contribution: bounds the truth-tier of interior claims.

reasoning.proof_standard: Quantum field theory in fixed curved backgrounds; gain insight by combining geometry with QFT modes.
questions: What do the field modes look like near this horizon?
What does an observer at infinity see, vs a freely falling observer?
Is there a thermodynamic analog of this geometric quantity?
failures: Treating QFT-in-curved-spacetime results as final theory of quantum gravity (they are not).

bibliography.citation: Hawking, S. W. (1974). Black hole explosions? Nature 248, 30.
Bardeen, J. M., Carter, B., Hawking, S. W. (1973). The four laws of black hole mechanics. Comm. Math. Phys. 31, 161.

apply.when: Reasoning about Hawking radiation, black-hole thermodynamics, the information paradox, or the boundary between classical GR and quantum gravity.
apply.cautions: Hawking radiation has not been observed. Any code path or visualization implying detection should be labeled speculative_extension.

contributions.title: Chandrasekhar limit
contributions.description: Showed that white dwarfs cannot exceed approximately 1.4 solar masses without collapsing; predicted the existence of objects beyond degeneracy pressure support.
contributions.relevance: Anchors stellar-mass black hole formation pathways.

contributions.title: The Mathematical Theory of Black Holes
contributions.description: Definitive monograph on the perturbation theory of black holes (Schwarzschild, Reissner-Nordstrom, Kerr).
contributions.relevance: Primary analytic reference for ISCO, photon sphere, quasi-normal modes, and perturbation methods.

reasoning.proof_standard: Push analytic methods (separation of variables, special functions, perturbation theory) as far as they will go before resorting to numerics.
questions: Does this problem admit a separable form?
What does perturbation theory tell us about stability?
What is the full hierarchy of governing equations?
failures: Sometimes pushing analytic methods past the point where numerics would be faster and clearer.

bibliography.citation: Chandrasekhar, S. (1983). The Mathematical Theory of Black Holes. Oxford University Press.
Chandrasekhar, S. (1931). The maximum mass of ideal white dwarfs. Astrophys. J. 74, 81.

apply.when: Implementing perturbation theory, quasi-normal modes, ringdown, or anything that admits a separable analytic form.
apply.cautions: His notation is dense. Translate carefully to modern conventions when implementing.

contributions.title: LIGO (co-founder)
contributions.description: Co-founded LIGO, the laser interferometer that made the first direct detection of gravitational waves in 2015.
contributions.relevance: Anchors the observational_constraint tier for the gravitational-wave catalog (GWTC) data we integrate.

contributions.title: Gravitation (with Misner and Wheeler)
contributions.description: Standard graduate textbook on general relativity.
contributions.relevance: Primary reference for the analytic formulas used throughout the project.

reasoning.proof_standard: Physical intuition first, then mathematics; relentless attention to observational consequences.
questions: How would you actually measure this?
What waveform does the detector see, and how much SNR do we need?
What is the experimentally relevant approximation?
failures: Occasionally overestimating the relevance of speculative ideas (e.g., wormholes for time travel) in popular communication.

bibliography.citation: Misner, C. W., Thorne, K. S., Wheeler, J. A. (1973). Gravitation. W. H. Freeman.
Abbott, B. P. et al. (LIGO Scientific Collaboration) (2016). Observation of gravitational waves from a binary black hole merger. Phys. Rev. Lett. 116, 061102.

apply.when: Designing observational consumers, gravitational-wave catalog integrations, or sanity-checking against MTW conventions.
apply.cautions: MTW uses (-+++) signature; document which signature any code path assumes.

contributions.title: Kerr-Newman solution
contributions.description: Generalized the Kerr solution to include electric charge, producing the most general black hole solution in Einstein-Maxwell theory.
contributions.relevance: Fourth metric in our DIM_METRIC table (after Schwarzschild, Kerr, Kerr-Newman, Kerr-deSitter).

contributions.title: Newman-Penrose formalism
contributions.description: Tetrad-based formulation of general relativity, well-suited for studying gravitational radiation and algebraically special spacetimes.
contributions.relevance: Useful for perturbation theory and ringdown modules.

reasoning.proof_standard: Use null tetrads and complex coordinates to expose hidden structure.
questions: What does this spacetime look like in a null tetrad frame?
Which Weyl scalars vanish, and what does that mean physically?
failures: The NP formalism is powerful but obscures intuition; do not impose it where Schwarzschild coordinates make a calculation trivial.

bibliography.citation: Newman, E. T., Janis, A. I. (1965). Note on the Kerr spinning-particle metric. J. Math. Phys. 6, 915.
Newman, E. T., Penrose, R. (1962). An approach to gravitational radiation by a method of spin coefficients. J. Math. Phys. 3, 566.

apply.when: Implementing charged black holes, the Kerr-Newman metric, or perturbation theory using Weyl scalars.
apply.cautions: Sign conventions vary between sources. Cite the convention you adopt.

contributions.title: Bekenstein entropy
contributions.description: Proposed that black holes carry entropy proportional to their horizon area: S = (k_B c^3 / 4 hbar G) A.
contributions.relevance: Defines the thermodynamic interpretation of black holes used in the Hawking-radiation pedagogy modules.

contributions.title: Bekenstein bound
contributions.description: Maximum amount of information that can be contained within a given region of space-time.
contributions.relevance: Speculative_extension territory. Useful for reasoning about holographic limits.

reasoning.proof_standard: Thermodynamic gedankenexperiments; use the second law as a constraint.
questions: What does the second law of thermodynamics say must happen here?
What information is hidden behind the horizon?
failures: Conflating well-established formulas (horizon area entropy) with conjectural bounds.

bibliography.citation: Bekenstein, J. D. (1973). Black holes and entropy. Phys. Rev. D 7, 2333.

apply.when: Designing thermodynamic or information-theoretic modules.
apply.cautions: Keep the analytic entropy formula separate from speculative quantum-gravity microstate counting.

contributions.title: Calabi-Yau manifolds
contributions.description: Proved the Calabi conjecture, establishing the existence of Ricci-flat Kähler manifolds, now central to string theory.
contributions.relevance: Speculative_extension; useful as a lens for reasoning about higher-dimensional generalizations.

contributions.title: Positive mass theorem
contributions.description: Proved (with Schoen) that the ADM mass of an asymptotically flat spacetime obeying the dominant energy condition is non-negative.
contributions.relevance: Bounds the analytic_classical claims about black-hole masses in the project.

reasoning.proof_standard: Rigorous global analysis on manifolds; PDE methods on geometric structures.
questions: What global PDE governs this geometric quantity?
What energy condition is implicitly assumed?
failures: Mathematical rigor sometimes outpaces physical relevance.

bibliography.citation: Schoen, R., Yau, S.-T. (1979). On the proof of the positive mass conjecture in general relativity. Comm. Math. Phys. 65, 45.

apply.when: Reviewing global claims about masses, energy conditions, or higher-dimensional generalizations.
apply.cautions: Calabi-Yau / string-theory connections are speculative_extension for this project.

contributions.title: Riemannian geometry
contributions.description: Generalized Gauss's surface geometry to arbitrary dimensions, introducing the metric tensor and the Riemann curvature tensor.
contributions.relevance: The mathematical language of general relativity. Every spacetime metric in this project is a Riemannian (technically pseudo-Riemannian, Lorentzian) metric.

contributions.title: Riemann surfaces
contributions.description: Multi-sheeted surfaces on which multi-valued complex functions become single-valued.
contributions.relevance: Useful for visualizing complex coordinate systems in Kerr geometry.

reasoning.proof_standard: Construct the right object for the question, then prove things about it.
questions: What is the right space on which this object is well-defined?
How does curvature constrain what can happen here?
failures: Riemann sometimes lacked formal rigor by modern standards; his arguments work but need cleaning.

bibliography.citation: Riemann, B. (1868). Uber die Hypothesen welche der Geometrie zu Grunde liegen. Abh. Gesellschaft Wiss. Gottingen 13.

apply.when: Any time you reach for a metric tensor, curvature, or differential geometry.
apply.cautions: Mind the signature convention; Riemannian (positive definite) vs Lorentzian (one minus sign) matters for causality.

contributions.title: Poincare conjecture (later proved by Perelman)
contributions.description: Topological characterization of the 3-sphere.
contributions.relevance: Indirectly relevant; topology of spatial slices matters for global GR claims.

contributions.title: Foundations of dynamical systems and chaos theory
contributions.description: Showed that the three-body problem can exhibit sensitive dependence on initial conditions, prefiguring chaos theory.
contributions.relevance: Directly relevant for Lyapunov-exponent and chaos modules in CHAOS_STATS.

contributions.title: Poincare sections
contributions.description: Technique for analyzing periodic and quasi-periodic orbits via successive intersections with a transversal surface.
contributions.relevance: Implemented as poincare_section_count in the TRAJECTORIES schema.

reasoning.proof_standard: Qualitative methods: identify invariants and topology before computing.
questions: Is the long-term behavior periodic, quasi-periodic, or chaotic?
What are the fixed points and their stability?
What is the topology of the phase space?
failures: Sometimes prioritized intuition over careful computation; verify quantitatively.

bibliography.citation: Poincare, H. (1890). Sur le probleme des trois corps et les equations de la dynamique. Acta Mathematica 13, 1.

apply.when: Analyzing trajectory chaos, ergodicity, or long-term stability of geodesics.
apply.cautions: Chaos diagnostics need long integrations and careful error control to be meaningful.

contributions.title: Intrinsic curvature (Theorema Egregium)
contributions.description: Showed that the Gaussian curvature of a surface is an intrinsic invariant, independent of embedding.
contributions.relevance: Foundation for the modern notion of curvature in Riemannian geometry, which GR inherits.

contributions.title: Method of least squares
contributions.description: Statistical estimation method for orbit determination and other inverse problems.
contributions.relevance: Useful for fitting model parameters to observational data (EHT, gravitational wave catalogs).

reasoning.proof_standard: Compute carefully and exhaustively; check special cases against the general result.
questions: What is intrinsic to this object, independent of how we describe it?
What does a careful asymptotic expansion say?
failures: Famously slow to publish, so others sometimes got the credit.

bibliography.citation: Gauss, C. F. (1828). Disquisitiones generales circa superficies curvas. Commentationes Soc. Reg. Sci. Gott. Recent. 6, 99.

apply.when: Anything involving intrinsic curvature, coordinate-independent geometric quantities, or parameter estimation.
apply.cautions: Modern conventions differ from Gauss's; translate carefully.

contributions.title: Cartan formalism (moving frames, exterior calculus)
contributions.description: Coordinate-free language for differential geometry using differential forms and orthonormal frames.
contributions.relevance: Modern formulation of general relativity uses Cartan structure equations; useful for tetrad-based numerical relativity.

contributions.title: Einstein-Cartan theory
contributions.description: Extension of general relativity including torsion as well as curvature.
contributions.relevance: Speculative_extension; useful as a foil for understanding why standard GR uses only curvature.

reasoning.proof_standard: Coordinate-free formulations; expose the geometric content first.
questions: What is the differential form that captures this physics?
Can I work in an orthonormal frame to simplify?
failures: Cartan's notation is famously dense; can obscure rather than clarify for beginners.

bibliography.citation: Cartan, E. (1923). Sur les varietes a connexion affine et la theorie de la relativite generalisee. Annales scientifiques de l'E.N.S. 40, 325.

apply.when: Implementing tetrad-based formulations or working with differential forms.
apply.cautions: Coordinate-free formulations are powerful but pedagogically heavy; document the equivalent coordinate calculation.

contributions.title: Local well-posedness of the Cauchy problem in GR
contributions.description: Proved that the Einstein equations admit a well-posed initial-value formulation in harmonic coordinates.
contributions.relevance: Foundation of numerical relativity. Every initial-value solver in the field traces back to this result.

reasoning.proof_standard: Rigorous PDE methods on the geometry of spacetime; pay close attention to function spaces and energy estimates.
questions: Is this problem well-posed?
What is the appropriate function space?
Do the energy estimates close?
failures: Mathematical rigor sometimes requires assumptions that physicists wave at.

bibliography.citation: Foures-Bruhat, Y. (1952). Theoreme d'existence pour certains systemes d'equations aux derivees partielles non lineaires. Acta Math. 88, 141.

apply.when: Designing initial-data and constraint-satisfaction algorithms for future numerical relativity work.
apply.cautions: Numerical methods must respect the constraint equations, not just the evolution equations.

contributions.title: 1919 eclipse expedition
contributions.description: Measured the deflection of starlight by the Sun, confirming Einstein's GR prediction.
contributions.relevance: Anchors the gravitational-lensing observational tier.

contributions.title: Eddington-Finkelstein coordinates
contributions.description: Coordinate system that removes the coordinate singularity at the Schwarzschild radius.
contributions.relevance: Useful for any module that needs to integrate geodesics across the event horizon.

contributions.title: Eddington limit
contributions.description: Upper limit on the luminosity of a stable, spherically symmetric accreting object.
contributions.relevance: Defines the natural scale for accretion-rate parameters in RUNS.

reasoning.proof_standard: Combine careful observation, physical reasoning, and clean coordinate choices.
questions: Is this prediction observationally testable?
What coordinate system makes this physics most transparent?
failures: Initially resisted Chandrasekhar's white-dwarf mass limit; let prior beliefs slow acceptance.

bibliography.citation: Dyson, F. W., Eddington, A. S., Davidson, C. (1920). A determination of the deflection of light by the Sun's gravitational field. Phil. Trans. Roy. Soc. A 220, 291.

apply.when: Working with horizon-crossing geodesics, accretion-rate normalization, or gravitational lensing observables.
apply.cautions: Eddington-limit accretion-rate constraints are approximations; real systems can exceed it in beamed or transient regimes.

contributions.title: Mass of Sgr A* via stellar orbits
contributions.description: Tracked stars orbiting the supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way, measuring its mass via Kepler's third law.
contributions.relevance: Anchors the observational_constraint tier for Sgr A* (~4.1 million solar masses).

reasoning.proof_standard: Long-baseline observational campaigns; track real objects over decades and let the data settle the theory.
questions: What is the simplest dynamical model that fits this orbit?
What systematics could fake this signal?
failures: Long-baseline programs are vulnerable to instrumentation changes.

bibliography.citation: Ghez, A. M. et al. (2008). Measuring distance and properties of the Milky Way's central supermassive black hole. Astrophys. J. 689, 1044.

apply.when: Using Sgr A* as an anchor for supermassive black hole parameters.
apply.cautions: Sgr A* parameters carry real uncertainties; quote them with error bars.

contributions.title: Sgr A* mass and proper motions (parallel program to Ghez)
contributions.description: Independent program tracking stars at the galactic center using VLT and GRAVITY interferometer.
contributions.relevance: Cross-validates Ghez's Sgr A* measurements with independent instruments.

reasoning.proof_standard: Push instrument capabilities to the diffraction limit; cross-check with independent observers.
questions: What is the systematic error from instrument calibration?
Does the independent observer see the same thing?
failures: Instrument-dependent calibration can introduce systematic offsets.

bibliography.citation: Genzel, R. et al. (2010). The galactic center massive black hole and nuclear star cluster. Rev. Mod. Phys. 82, 3121.

apply.when: Same as Ghez. Use both as independent observational anchors.
apply.cautions: Cross-check parameter values between Ghez and Genzel programs; they should agree within stated uncertainties.

contributions.title: Event Horizon Telescope founding director
contributions.description: Led the EHT collaboration that produced the first horizon-scale images of M87* (2019) and Sgr A* (2022).
contributions.relevance: Provides the observational_constraint reference for the horizon-scale ring (M87*: 42 +/- 3 microarcseconds; Sgr A*: 51.8 +/- 2.3 microarcseconds).

reasoning.proof_standard: Push interferometric baselines to Earth-diameter scale; use closure quantities (closure phase, closure amplitude) that are robust to station errors.
questions: What is the closure phase of the source brightness distribution?
Is the ring asymmetric, and what does that tell us about the spin axis?
failures: Imaging at the diffraction limit is sensitive to prior assumptions; EHT collaboration carefully cross-validates with multiple algorithms.

bibliography.citation: Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration (2019). First M87 Event Horizon Telescope results. I-VI. Astrophys. J. Lett. 875.

apply.when: Using horizon-scale imaging data as a constraint or validation target.
apply.cautions: Reported uncertainties are non-Gaussian; do not naively convolve them as if they were.

contributions.title: Strominger-Vafa microstate counting
contributions.description: Counted the microstates of certain extremal black holes in string theory and reproduced the Bekenstein-Hawking entropy.
contributions.relevance: Speculative_extension. Defines the bar for what a microscopic explanation of black-hole entropy would look like.

contributions.title: Soft hair on black holes
contributions.description: Argued (with Hawking and Perry) that black holes carry an infinite number of soft hairs (low-energy gauge or graviton excitations).
contributions.relevance: Speculative_extension; relevant for the information paradox discussion.

reasoning.proof_standard: Use stringy / holographic dualities to translate intractable problems into computable ones.
questions: Is there a dual description in which this is easy?
What symmetries are hidden by gauge choice?
failures: Holographic reasoning assumes string theory's framework; physically relevant conclusions need to be checked against model-independent constraints.

bibliography.citation: Strominger, A., Vafa, C. (1996). Microscopic origin of the Bekenstein-Hawking entropy. Phys. Lett. B 379, 99.

apply.when: Reasoning about microstate counting, soft hair, or holographic dualities.
apply.cautions: Label everything in this lineage as speculative_extension until cross-validated against observation.
